18 And for a period of forty years he suffered their manners in the wilderness.

19 And after casting down seven nations in the land of Chanaan, he divided their land to them by lot.

20 After these things, for about four hundred and fifty years, he gave judges until Samuel the prophet.

21 From there they asked for a king, and God gave them Saul son of Cis, a man from the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years.

22 After removing him, he raised up David to be their king, about whom he testified and said, 'I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after my heart, who will do all my will.'

23 From this man's seed, according to promise, God raised up a Savior for Israel, Jesus.

24 Before his entrance, John had first preached a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.

25 As John was fulfilling his course, he said, "Who do you think me to be? I am not he. But see, one is coming after me, the sandal of whose foot I am not worthy to loose."

26 Men, brothers, sons of the family of Abraham, and those among you who fear God — to you this word of salvation has been sent.

27 For those dwelling in Jerusalem and their rulers, not knowing this man nor the voices of the prophets that are read every Sabbath, fulfilled them by judging him.

28 And having found no cause for death, they asked Pilate to have him killed.

29 And when they had finished all that was written about him, they took him down from the wood and put him into a tomb.

30 But God raised him from the dead.

31 He was seen over many days by those who had come up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are his witnesses to the people.

32 We are preaching the gospel to you, the promise that was made to the Fathers, that God has fulfilled this for us their children, raising up Jesus.

33 As it has also been written in the second psalm, "You are my Son; today I have begotten you."

34 Now that he raised him from the dead, no longer about to return to corruption, he said in this way: I will give you the holy and faithful things of David.

35 Therefore he says also in another place, "You will not let your Holy One see corruption."

36 David, having ministered to his own generation by the counsel of God, fell asleep, and was added to his fathers, and saw corruption.

37 But the one whom God raised did not see corruption.

38 Therefore let it be known to you, men and brothers, that through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you.

39 and from all things from which you were not able to be justified by the law of Moses, by this one everyone who believes is justified.

40 Look, then, so that what has been said in the prophets may not come upon you.

41 See, you despisers, and marvel and become disfigured, because I am working a work in your days — a work that you would never believe if someone declares it to you.

42 As they were going out of the synagogue of the Jews, the Gentiles begged that these words be spoken to them on the next Sabbath.

43 When the synagogue was dismissed, many of the Jews and of the worshipping proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas, who, speaking to them, persuaded them to continue in the grace of God.

44 On the coming Sabbath, almost all the city gathered to hear the word of God.

45 When the Jews saw the crowds, they were filled with zeal and spoke against the things being said by Paul, speaking against and blaspheming.

46 So Paul and Barnabas spoke boldly, saying, "It was necessary that the word of God be spoken to you first; since you thrust it away and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life— see, we are turning to the nations."

47 For thus the Lord has commanded us: 'I have put you as a light for the nations, for you to be for salvation to the last part of the earth.'

48 When the nations heard this, they rejoiced and glorified the word of the Lord, and as many as had been appointed to eternal life believed.

49 The word of the Lord was being spread throughout the whole region.

50 But the Jews stirred up the worshipping women and the honorable women and the leading men of the city, and raised a persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and cast them out of their borders.

51 But they, having shaken off the dust of their feet against them, came to Iconium.

52 And the disciples were filled with joy and the Holy Spirit.


## Chapter 14

1 It happened in Iconium that they together entered into the synagogue of the Jews and spoke in such a way that a great multitude of both Jews and Greeks believed.

2 But the disobedient Jews raised up and harmed the souls of the nations against the brothers.

3 So for a considerable time they stayed, speaking boldly in the Lord, who testified to the word of his grace and granted signs and wonders to be performed through their hands.

4 And the multitude of the city was split, and some were with the Jews and some with the apostles.

5 When the assault of both the nations and the Jews with their rulers happened, to mistreat and stone them.

6 Having considered this, they fled to the cities of Lycaonia—Lystra and Derbe—and the surrounding region.

7 And there they were preaching the gospel.

8 And a man in Lystra, impossible in his feet, sat, being lame from his mother's belly, who had never walked.

9 This man heard Paul speaking, who gazed at him and saw that he has faith to be saved.

10 He said with a great voice, "Rise upright on your feet," and he leaped and walked.

11 The crowds, seeing what Paul did, lifted up their voices, saying in Lycaonian, "The gods, having become like men, have come down to us."

12 They were calling Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, because he himself was the leader of the message.

13 The priest of Zeus, who was before their city, brought bulls and garlands to the gates and wanted to sacrifice with the crowds.

14 When the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard this, they tore their garments and ran into the crowd, crying out.

15 "Men, why are you doing these things? We also are like-natured men, preaching the gospel to you to turn back from these vain things to the living God, who made the heaven, the earth, the sea, and all that is in them."

16 In past generations he allowed all the nations to go their own ways.

17 Yet he did not leave himself without witness, doing good—giving us rains from heaven and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness.

18 And saying these things, they scarcely caused the crowds to rest from sacrificing to them.

19 But Jews from Antioch and Iconium came upon them, and having persuaded the crowds and stoned Paul, they dragged him outside the city, supposing him to be dead.

20 When the disciples surrounded him, he rose and entered the city, and the next day he went out with Barnabas to Derbe.

21 And after preaching the gospel to that city and making many disciples, they returned to Lystra and to Iconium and to Antioch.

22 strengthening the souls of the disciples, exhorting them to continue in the faith, and saying that through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God.

23 When they had chosen elders for them in each church, having prayed with fasting, they committed them to the Lord in whom they had believed.

24 After passing through Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia.

25 After speaking the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia.

26 From there they sailed away to Antioch, where they had been delivered to the grace of God for the work which they had fulfilled.

27 When they had come and gathered the church together, they announced all that God had done with them and that he had opened a door of faith to the nations.

28 They stayed there for no little time with the disciples.


## Chapter 15

1 And some men came down from Judea and were teaching the brothers that unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses, you cannot be saved.

2 After a dissension and not a few disputation had happened, Paul and Barnabas—along with some others among them—were appointed to go up to the apostles and elders in Jerusalem about this question.

3 Those who were escorted by the church passed through Phoenicia and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the nations, and they caused great joy to all the brothers.

4 When they arrived at Jerusalem, they were welcomed by the church and the apostles and the elders, and they announced all that God had done with them.

5 But some of those from the sect of the Pharisees who had believed rose up, saying that they must circumcise them and command them to keep the law of Moses.

6 The apostles and the elders gathered to see about this word.

7 After much disputing, Peter rose and said to them, "Men and brothers, you know that from early days God chose among us that by my mouth the nations should hear the word of the gospel and believe."

8 And God, who knows the hearts, testified to them by giving them the Holy Spirit, just as He also did to us.

9 He made no distinction between us and them, having cleansed their hearts by faith.

10 Now therefore why do you test God by placing a yoke on the neck of the disciples which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear?

11 But through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, we believe that we will be saved in the same way as they also are.

12 All the multitude became silent, and they heard Barnabas and Paul declaring how many signs and wonders God had done among the nations through them.

13 After they became silent, James answered, saying, "Men, brothers, listen to me.

14 Simeon declared how first God visited to take from the nations a people for his name.

15 And with this the words of the prophets agree, as it is written.

16 After these things I will return and rebuild the fallen tabernacle of David; I will build again its ruins and lift it up.

17 so that the residue of men may seek out the Lord, and all the nations upon whom my name is called, says the Lord who does all these things.

18 All his works are known to God from eternity.

19 Therefore I judge not to trouble those who from among the nations turn back to God.

20 instead write to them to abstain from the pollutions of the idols and the fornication and the strangled and the blood.

21 For Moses from generations of old has in every city those who preach him, being read in the synagogues every Sabbath.

22 Then it seemed good to the apostles and the elders, with the whole church, to choose men from among them and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas—Judas who is called Barsabas, and Silas, men considered leaders among the brothers.

23 Having written by their hand these things: the apostles and the elders and the brothers, to the brothers from among the nations in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia, greetings.

24 We have heard that some from among us have gone out and troubled you with words, subverting your souls, saying to be circumcised and to keep the law, to whom we gave no such command.

25 It seemed good to us, having come together unanimously, to choose men and send them to you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul.

26 to men who have delivered their souls for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.

27 Therefore we have sent Judas and Silas, who themselves will report the same things by word.

28 For it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay on you no greater burden than these necessary things.

29 Abstain from things offered to idols, and from blood, and from strangled, and from fornication; from which keeping yourselves, you will do well. Farewell.

30 So those who had been released came to Antioch, and gathering the multitude together, they gave the letter.

31 Having read it, they rejoiced at the comfort.

32 Judas and Silas, who were themselves prophets, encouraged and strengthened the brothers with many words.

33 After they had spent some time, they were sent away in peace from the brothers to the apostles.

34 But it seemed good to Silas to continue there.

35 But Paul and Barnabas stayed in Antioch, teaching and preaching the gospel, along with many others, the word of the Lord.

36 After some days Paul said to Barnabas, "Let us turn back and visit our brothers in every city where we proclaimed the word of the Lord, to see how they are."

37 But Barnabas planned to take along John, who is called Mark.

38 But Paul considered it worthy not to take with them this one who had departed from them from Pamphylia and did not come together with them to the work.

39 Then the contention occurred so that they departed from one another, and Barnabas, taking Mark along, sailed away to Cyprus.

40 Paul, having called Silas, went out, having been delivered to the grace of God by the brothers.

41 He was passing through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches.


## Chapter 16

1 Then he arrived at Derbe and Lystra, and see, a certain disciple was there named Timothy, the son of a faithful Jewish woman but of a Greek father.

2 He was testified of by the brothers in Lystra and Iconium.

3 Paul wanted this man to go out with him, and taking him, he circumcised him because of the Jews who were in those places, for they all knew that his father was Greek.

4 As they were passing through the cities, they delivered the decrees that had been judged by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem for them to keep.

5 The churches therefore were established in faith and abounded in number daily.

6 When they had passed through the region of Phrygia and Galatia, they were forbidden by the Holy Spirit to speak the word in Asia.

7 When they had come up to Mysia, they attempted to go into Bithynia, but the Spirit did not allow them.

8 And after they had passed through Mysia, they went down to Troas.

9 A vision appeared to Paul during the night: a Macedonian man was standing there, imploring him and saying, "Come over to Macedonia and help us."

10 As soon as he saw the vision, at once we sought to go out into Macedonia, concluding that the Lord had called us to preach the gospel to them.

11 When we had set sail from Troas, we ran a straight course to Samothrace, and on the following day to Neapolis.

12 From there we went to Philippi, which is the first city of the district of Macedonia, a colony; and we stayed in that city for some days.

13 On the day of the Sabbaths we went out outside the city beside a river, where a place of prayer was supposed to be, and sitting down we spoke to the women who had come together.

14 A woman named Lydia, a seller of purple from the city of Thyatira, who worshiped God, was listening, and the Lord opened her heart to give heed to what was spoken by Paul.

15 When she was baptized, and her household, she urged us, saying, "If you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, enter into my house and remain," and she constrained us.

16 And it happened, as we were going to the place of prayer, that a certain maidservant who had a spirit of divination met us, who provided much profit to her masters by divining.

17 This woman followed Paul and us, crying out and saying, "These men are servants of the Most High God, who proclaim to you a way of salvation."

18 This he kept doing for many days; but Paul, having been grieved and having turned back in the Spirit, said, "I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to go out from her," and it went out that very hour.

19 When her masters saw that their hope of profit had gone out, they took hold of Paul and Silas and drew them into the marketplace before the rulers.

20 having brought them to the captains, they said, "These men, being Jews, are exceedingly troubling our city."

21 They proclaim customs that it is not lawful for us to receive or practice, being Romans.

22 The crowd also rose up together against them, and the captains, having torn off their garments, commanded to beat them with rods.

23 After laying many plagues on them, they cast them into prison, commanding the jailor to keep them securely.

24 having received such a command, he cast them into the inner prison and made their feet secure in the wood.

25 About midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God, and the prisoners were listening to them.

26 Suddenly a great earthquake happened, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken; immediately all the doors were opened, and all the bonds were released.

27 After the jailor woke up from sleep and saw the prison doors open, he drew his sword and was about to kill himself, supposing the prisoners had escaped.

28 But Paul called out with a loud voice, saying, "Do no evil to yourself, for we are all here."

29 And having asked for lights, he ran in and, trembling, fell before Paul and Silas.

30 Then, bringing them out, he said, "Sirs, what must I do to be saved?"

31 But they said, "Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and you will be saved, you and your household."

32 And they spoke the word of the Lord to him and to all those in his house.

33 Receiving them in that hour of the night, he washed them from the plagues, and he was baptized immediately, he and all his.

34 After he led them up into his house, he set a table before them, and he rejoiced with all his household, having believed in God.

35 When day became, the captains sent the serjeants saying, "Release those men."

36 The jailor reported these words to Paul, saying, "The captains have sent word that you may be released. Now therefore go out and go in peace."

37 But Paul said to them, "After beating us publicly, uncondemned men who are Romans, they cast us into prison; and now do they cast us out secretly? No indeed, but let them come themselves and lead us out."

38 The serjeants announced these words to the captains, and they feared when they heard that they are Romans.

39 And when they came, they appealed to them, and after leading them out, they asked them to go out of the city.

40 They went out of the prison and entered Lydia, and seeing the brothers, they encouraged them and went out.


## Chapter 17

1 And after going throughout Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.

2 As was his custom, Paul entered and reasoned with them from the Scriptures for three Sabbaths.

3 Opening and demonstrating that the Christ must suffer and rise from the dead, and that this Jesus is the Christ whom I proclaim to you.

4 Some of them were persuaded and consorted with Paul and Silas, as did a great multitude of the worshiping Greeks and not a few of the leading women.

5 The disobedient Jews, having become zealous and receiving some evil men from the marketplace idlers, and having gathered a company, were troubling the city; and standing by the house of Jason, they were seeking to lead them to the crowd.

6 But when they did not find them, they dragged Jason and some brothers before the rulers of the city, crying out, "These men who have troubled the world have also come here present.

7 Jason has received them, and these all are acting contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, Jesus.

8 But they troubled the crowd and the rulers of the city when they heard these things.

9 After taking a sufficient bond from Jason and the rest, they released them.

10 And the brothers immediately sent away both Paul and Silas through the night to Berea, who, when they came, went away into the synagogue of the Jews.

11 Now these were more noble than those in Thessalonica, for they received the word with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see if these things were so.

12 Therefore many of them believed, and not a few of the honorable Greek women and men.

13 When the Jews from Thessalonica knew that the word of God was also proclaimed by Paul in Berea, they came there as well, shaking the crowds.

14 Immediately at that time the brothers sent forth Paul to go as far as the sea, but Silas and Timothy remained there.

15 Those who were appointing Paul led him as far as Athens, and after taking a commandment to Silas and Timothy that they should come to him with all speed, they went out.

16 Now while Paul was waiting for them in Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he observed that the city was wholly given to idolatry.

17 He reasoned, therefore, in the synagogue with the Jews and the worshippers, and in the marketplace every day with those who met with him.

18 Now some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers encountered him, and some said, "What could this babbler mean to say?" while others said, "He seems to be a setter forth of strange demons," because he preached the gospel of Jesus and the resurrection to them.

19 Taking hold of him, they led him to the Areopagus, saying, "Are we able to know what this new teaching is that is spoken by you?"

20 For you are bringing some entertaining things to our ears; therefore we want to know what these things mean to be.

21 Now all the Athenians and the strangers dwelling there had no other opportunity than to say or hear something new.

22 Standing in the middle of the Areopagus, Paul said, "Men of Athens, I behold that you are too superstitious in all things."

23 For as I was passing through and observing your objects of devotion, I found also an altar with this inscription: TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. Therefore, what you worship in ignorance, this I proclaim to you.

24 The God who did the world and all the things in it—He, existing as Lord of heaven and earth—does not dwell in temples made by hands.

25 Nor is he healed by human hands, as though he needed anything, since he himself gives to all life and breath in all things.

26 He made from one blood all the nation of men to dwell on all the face of the earth, having determined appointed times and the boundaries of their habitation.

27 They should seek the Lord, in the hope that they might feel after him and find him, though he is not far from each one of us.

28 In him we live and move and have our being, as also some of your own poets have said, 'For we are indeed his offspring.'

29 Since we are God's kind, we ought not to suppose that the divine nature is like gold or silver or stone, a mark of human art and thought.

30 Therefore, having overlooked the times of ignorance, God now commands all men everywhere to repent.

31 because he has set a day on which he is about to judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has determined, having provided assurance to all by raising him from the dead.

32 When they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked, but others said, "We will hear you again about this."

33 And so Paul went out from their midst.

34 But some men joined him and believed, among whom also were Dionysius the Areopagite, and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.


## Chapter 18

1 After these things, Paul separated from Athens and came to Corinth.

2 And when he found a certain Jew named Aquila, a Pontian by race, lately come from Italy with Priscilla his wife—because Claudius had appointed all the Jews to depart from Rome—he went to them.

3 And because he was of the same craft, he stayed with them and worked, for by trade they were tent-makers.

4 He reasoned in the synagogue every Sabbath, and he persuaded Jews and Greeks.

5 When Silas and Timothy came down from Macedonia, Paul was constrained by the Spirit as he testified to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ.

6 But when they resisted and blasphemed, he shook off his garments and said to them, "Your blood be on your own head! I am clean. From now on I will go to the nations."

7 Having departed from there, he came into the house of a man named Justus, who worshipped God, whose house was joined hard to the synagogue.

8 Crispus, the synagogue ruler, believed in the Lord together with his whole house, and many of the Corinthians, hearing, believed and were baptized.

9 The Lord said to Paul in a vision at night, "Do not fear, but speak and do not be silent."

10 For I am with you, and no one will lay a hand on you to harm you, because I have many people in this city.

11 He sat there for a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.

12 While Gallio was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews made insurrection unanimously against Paul and led him to the judgment seat.

13 They say that this man persuades people to worship God contrary to the law.

14 As Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Jews, "If indeed it were a matter of evil doing or wicked lewdness, O Jews, I would have borne with you in a reasonable way."

15 If there is a question about a word and names and your own law, you yourselves will see to it; for I do not will to be a judge of these matters.

16 And he drove them from the judgment seat.

17 Then all the Greeks took hold of Sosthenes, the synagogue ruler, and struck him in front of the judgment seat, and none of these things mattered to Gallio.

18 But after remaining with the brothers for a sufficient number of days, Paul bid them farewell and sailed for Syria, and with him were Priscilla and Aquila, having sheared his head in Cenchrea, for he had a prayer vow.

19 He arrived at Ephesus, and left them there; and he himself, having entered the synagogue, reasoned with the Jews.

20 But when they asked him to remain with them for a longer time, he did not consent.

21 He bid them farewell, saying, "I must by all means keep the coming feast in Jerusalem; but I will return again to you, God willing." And he was led up from Ephesus.

22 And when he had gone down to Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church, and then went down to Antioch.

23 After doing some time, he went out, passing through the region of Galatia and Phrygia in order, strengthening all the disciples.

24 A Jew named Apollos, an Alexandrian by birth, an eloquent man, arrived at Ephesus, being able in the Scriptures.

25 He was instructed in the way of the Lord and, being fervent in the Spirit, spoke and taught carefully about the things concerning the Lord, knowing only the baptism of John.

26 He began to speak boldly in the synagogue, and when Aquila and Priscilla heard him, they received him and expounded to him the way of God more carefully.

27 When he willed to pass through into Achaia, the brothers exhorted and wrote to the disciples to welcome him; having come, he greatly contributed to those who had believed through grace.

28 For he mightily convinced the Jews publicly, showing through the Scriptures that Jesus was the Christ.


## Chapter 19

1 Now it happened that while Apollos was in Corinth, Paul passed through the upper parts and came to Ephesus, and he found some disciples.

2 He said to them, "Did you take the Holy Spirit when you believed?" And they said to him, "We have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit."

3 And he said to them, "Into what then were you baptized?" And they said, "Into John's baptism."

4 Paul said, "John baptized with a baptism of repentance, saying to the people to believe in the one coming after him, that is, in Christ Jesus."

5 And having heard, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.

6 When Paul had laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came upon them, and they spoke in tongues and prophesied.

7 And all the men were about twelve.

8 After he entered the synagogue, he spoke boldly for three months, reasoning and persuading about the kingdom of God.

9 But when some were hardened and disobedient, reviling the Way before the multitude, he departed from them and separated the disciples, reasoning daily in the school of a certain Tyrannus.

10 This happened for two years, so that all those dwelling in Asia heard the word of the Lord Jesus, both Jews and Greeks.

11 God kept on doing extraordinary miracles by the hands of Paul.

12 so that even handkerchiefs or aprons were brought from his skin to those who were weak, and the diseases were delivered from them, and the evil spirits went out from them.

13 Then some of the traveling Jewish exorcists attempted to name over those who had the evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, "We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preaches."

14 There were seven sons of Sceva, a Jewish high priest, who were doing this.

15 And the evil spirit answered and said, "I know Jesus and I am acquainted with Paul, but who are you?"

16 The man in whom the evil spirit was leaped on them and, having overpowered them, prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded.

17 This became known to all Jews and Greeks dwelling in Ephesus, and fear fell upon them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified.

18 Many of those who had believed also came, confessing and announcing their deeds.

19 Many of those who practiced magic arts brought their books together and burned them in the sight of all, and they reckoned up the value of them and found it to be fifty thousand pieces of silver.

20 Thus the word of the Lord was growing with force and power.

21 Now when these things were fulfilled, Paul put in his Spirit to go after passing through Macedonia and Achaia to Jerusalem, saying, "After I have been there, I must also see Rome."

22 Having sent two of those who served him, Timothy and Erastus, into Macedonia, he himself stayed for a time in Asia.

23 Now at that time there happened no small stir about the way.

24 For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith who made silver temples of Artemis, provided no small profit to the craftsmen.

25 Having gathered together these men and the workers of such trades, he said, "Men, you know that our wealth comes from this profit."

26 Behold and hear that not only at Ephesus, but almost all of Asia, this Paul, having persuaded a sufficient crowd, has removed them, saying that they are not gods which are made by hands.

27 Not only is this part in danger of coming to nought for us, but also the temple of the great goddess Artemis is about to be reckoned for nothing, and her majesty is about even to be cast down, whom the whole province of Asia and the world worship.

28 And when they heard this, they became full of wrath and cried out, saying, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"

29 The whole city was filled with confusion, and they rushed unanimously into the spectacle, having caught Gaius and Aristarchus, Macedonians, travelling companions of Paul.

30 But when Paul willed to enter the crowd, the disciples did not allow him.

31 And some of the chief men of Asia, being friends of his, sent to him and begged him not to give himself up to the spectacle.

32 So some were crying out one thing and others another, for the assembly was in confusion, and the majority did not know why they had come together.

33 The crowd put forward Alexander, the Jews having put him forward, but Alexander, having beckoned with his hand, wanted to defend himself to the crowd.

34 When they recognized that he was a Jew, one voice arose from all, crying out for about two hours, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"

35 Having appeased the crowd, the scribe says, "Men of Ephesus, what man is there who does not know that the city of the Ephesians is a worshipper of the great goddess Artemis and of the image which fell down from Jupiter?

36 Since these things cannot be spoken against, it must be that you are appeased and practice nothing heady.

37 For you have led these men here, who are neither robbers of churches nor blasphemers of your goddess.

38 If therefore Demetrius and the craftsmen with him have a case against anyone, the courts are open and the proconsuls are present; let them accuse one another.

39 If, however, you seek anything about other matters, it will be determined in the lawful church.

40 For we are in danger of being accused of dissension concerning today's events, since no cause exists for which we will be able to give an account of this conspiracy.

41 And having said these things, he released the church.


## Chapter 20

1 After the uproar had ceased, Paul called the disciples, and after greeting them, he went out to go into Macedonia.

2 And after passing through those parts and exhorting them with many words, he came into Greece.

3 Having done three months, when a plot happened against him by the Jews as he was about to be led up into Syria, he resolved to return through Macedonia.

4 Sopater of Berea accompanied him as far as Asia, and of the Thessalonians, Aristarchus and Secundus, and Gaius of Derbe and Timothy, and of Asia, Tychicus and Trophimus.

5 They went ahead and waited for us in Troas.

6 We sailed after the days of Unleavened Bread from Philippi and came to them at Troas in five days, where we stayed seven days.

7 Now on the first day of the week, when the disciples were gathered together to break bread, Paul reasoned with them, intending to depart the next day, and he continued his speech until midnight.

8 Now there were sufficient lamps in the upper chamber where they were gathered.

9 A young man named Eutychus was sitting at the window, being weighed down by a deep sleep as Paul reasoned at length; having been overcome by sleep, he fell down from the third loft and was taken up dead.

10 After going down, Paul fell upon him and, embracing him, said, "Do not be troubled, for his soul is in him."

11 So he went up, broke bread, tasted it, and after talking for a long time until daybreak, he left.

12 And they led the live servant, and were encouraged not a little.

13 But we, going before to the boat, led up to Assos, from there being about to take up Paul, for he had been appointed, being about to go on foot.

14 When he encountered us at Assos, we took him on board and came to Mitylene.

15 Sailing away from there, on the following day we arrived opposite Chios, and on the next day we arrived at Samos; and after remaining at Trogyllium, on the following day we came to Miletus.

16 For Paul had decided to sail by Ephesus so that it would not happen for him to spend time in Asia, for he was hastening, if it were possible for him, to be in Jerusalem on the day of Pentecost.

17 From Miletus, having sent to Ephesus, he summoned the elders of the church.

18 When they came to him, he said to them, "You know from the first day on which I embarked for Asia how I was with you the whole time.

19 serving the Lord with all humility and with many tears and with trials that happened to me through the plots of the Jews.

20 I did not draw back from announcing to you and teaching you publicly and from house to house those things which are profitable.

21 testifying to both Jews and Greeks about repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ.

22 And now, see, I am bound in the Spirit, going to Jerusalem, not knowing the things that will meet me there.

23 Except that the Holy Spirit testifies in every city, saying that bonds and tribulations remain for me.

24 But I do not make any word of account, nor do I hold my soul precious to myself, so as to complete my course with joy and the ministry that I took from the Lord Jesus, to testify to the gospel of the grace of God.

25 And now see, I know that you will no longer see my face—all of you among whom I passed through preaching the kingdom of God.

26 Therefore I testify to you on this present day that I am clean from the blood of all.

27 For I did not draw back from announcing to you all the counsel of God.

28 Give heed to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has put you as overseers to shepherd the church of God, which He purchased with His own blood.

29 For I know this, that after my departing heavy wolves will enter among you, not sparing the flock.

30 And from among your own selves will rise men speaking perverse things to draw away the disciples after them.

31 Therefore watch, remembering that for a space of three years, night and day, I did not cease with tears to admonish each one.

32 And now I commit you, brothers, to God and to the word of his grace, which is able to build you up and to give you an inheritance among all those who are sanctified.

33 I desired no one's silver or gold or clothing.

34 You yourselves know that these hands ministered to my needs and to those who were with me.

35 I showed you all things, that by laboring in this way you must help the weak and remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said, "It is more blessed to give than to receive."

36 And having said these things, he knelt down and prayed with all of them.

37 There was sufficient weeping from all, and falling upon Paul's neck, they kissed him.

38 They were especially anguished over the word he had spoken, that they were about to see his face no more, and they escorted him to the boat.


## Chapter 21

1 When it happened, after we had been led up and had withdrawn from them, we came with a straight course to Cos, and on the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara.

2 And having found a boat crossing over to Phoenix, we embarked and were led away.

3 After appearing, we left Cyprus behind and sailed on the left to Syria, and were brought down to Tyre, for there the boat was unlading its burden.

4 Having found the disciples, we continued there seven days; they kept telling Paul through the Spirit not to go up to Jerusalem.

5 But when it happened that we had accomplished the days, going out we were going, all escorting us with wives and children until outside of the city; and putting our knees on the shore, we prayed.

6 And after we had greeted one another, we embarked into the boat, and those returned to their own homes.

7 After finishing the voyage from Tyre, we arrived at Ptolemais, and after greeting the brothers, we stayed one day with them.

8 On the next day, those around Paul went out and came to Caesarea, and entering the house of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the seven, we remained with him.

9 This man had four virgin daughters who were prophesying.

10 But while we were continuing on for many days, a certain prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.

11 And when he had come to us and taken Paul's belt, having bound his own hands and feet, he said, "Thus says the Holy Spirit: 'The man whose belt this is—so will the Jews in Jerusalem bind him and deliver him into the hands of the nations.'"

12 When we heard these things, both we and the local people begged him not to go up to Jerusalem.

13 Then Paul answered, "What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart? For I am ready not only to be bound, but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus."

14 But when he was not being persuaded, we fell silent, saying, "Let the will of the Lord be done."

15 After these days we packed up and went up to Jerusalem.

16 Some of the disciples from Caesarea also came with us, leading us to the house of Mnason, a Cypriot and an old disciple, with whom we were to be entertained.

17 When we had arrived at Jerusalem, the brothers gladly received us.

18 On the following day Paul went in with us to James, and all the elders came.

19 And after greeting them, he declared one by one what God had done among the nations through his ministry.

20 When they heard this, they glorified the Lord and said to him, "Behold, brother, how many myriads of Jews there are who have believed, and all are zealots of the law."

21 They have been instructed about you, that you teach all the Jews who are among the Gentiles to forsake Moses, saying they should not circumcise their children nor walk according to the customs.

22 What, then, is it? Surely a multitude must come together, for they will hear that you have come.

23 Therefore do this that we say to you: there are four men among us having a prayer upon themselves.

24 Receive these men, purify yourself along with them, and spend on them so that they may shave their heads, and all will know that there is nothing to what they have been instructed about you, but that you yourself also walk orderly, keeping the law.

25 Concerning the nations who have believed, we have written, judging that they should keep no such thing, except to keep themselves from what is offered to idols, and from blood, and from what is strangled, and from fornication.

26 Then Paul, having received the men, on the next day, having been purified with them, went into the temple, declaring the accomplishment of the days of purification until the offering was offered for each one of them.

27 But as the seven days were about to be completed, the Jews from Asia, having beheld him in the temple, confounded all the crowd and laid hands on him.

28 Crying out, "Men of Israel, help! This is the man who teaches all men everywhere against the people and the law and this place, and also has brought Greeks into the temple and has defiled this holy place."

29 They had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him, and they supposed that Paul had brought him into the temple.

30 The whole city was moved, and a concourse of the people took place; and taking hold of Paul, they drew him outside the temple, and immediately the doors were shut.

31 But as they were seeking to kill him, tidings went up to the captain of the cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion.

32 Immediately receiving soldiers and centurions, he ran down to them, and when they saw the captain and the soldiers, they ceased striking Paul.

33 Then the captain drew near, took hold of him, and commanded him to be bound with two chains, and asked who he might be and what he had done.

34 But some were crying out one thing, others another among the crowd; and not being able to know the certainty because of the uproar, he commanded him to be led into the camp.

35 But when it happened that he was at the steps, he was borne by the soldiers because of the violence of the crowd.

36 For the multitude of the people followed, crying out, "Take him."

37 As he was about to be brought into the camp, Paul said to the captain, "Is it lawful for me to say something to you?" And he said, "Do you know Greek?"

38 Are you not then the Egyptian who before these days troubled and led out into the wilderness the four thousand men of the assassins?

39 Then Paul said, "I am a man, a Jew, of Tarsus, a citizen of no mean city of Cilicia; and I beg you, permit me to speak to the people."

40 After he permitted it, Paul, standing on the stairs, beckoned with his hand to the people; and when great silence occurred, he called to them in the Hebrew language, saying,


## Chapter 22

1 Men, brothers and fathers, hear my defense which is now before you.

2 And when they heard that he was calling to them in the Hebrew language, they provided all the more quietness; and he says,

3 I am a Jewish man, born in Tarsus of Cilicia, but brought up in this city at the feet of Gamaliel, disciplined in the perfect manner of the ancestral law, being a zealot for God, just as all of you are today.

4 who persecuted this Way to the death, binding and delivering both men and women to prisons.

5 even as the high priest testifies for me, and all the elderhood; from whom also, having received letters to the brothers, I was going to Damascus to lead even those who were there bound to Jerusalem, so that they might be punished.

6 As I was going and drawing near to Damascus, about noon suddenly from heaven a sufficient light shone around me.

7 I fell to the ground and heard a voice saying to me, "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?"

8 I answered, "Who are you, Lord?" And he said to me, "I am Jesus the Nazarene, whom you are persecuting."

9 Now those who were with me beheld the light, and became terrified, but did not hear the voice of the one speaking to me.

10 So I said, 'What shall I do, Lord?' And the Lord said to me, 'Rise and go into Damascus, and there you will be told about everything that has been appointed for you to do.'

11 And as I could not look at the glory of that light, being led by the hand by those who were with me, I came into Damascus.

12 Now a certain man named Ananias, a devout man according to the law, was testified to by all the Jews living there.

13 Coming to me and standing by, he said to me, "Saul, brother, receive your sight." And in that very hour I received my sight and looked at him.

14 But he said, 'The God of our fathers has chosen you to know his will and to see the Righteous One and to hear a voice from his mouth.'

15 for you will be a witness to him for all people of what you have seen and heard.

16 And now, why are you about to delay? Rise, be baptized and wash away your sins, calling upon the name of the Lord.

17 It happened to me when I had returned to Jerusalem and while I was praying in the temple, that I fell into a trance.

18 and see him saying to me, 'Hasten and go out quickly from Jerusalem, because they will not receive your testimony about me.'

19 I also said, Lord, they themselves know that I was imprisoning and beating throughout the synagogues those who believe in you.

20 And when the blood of Stephen your witness was poured out, I myself was standing by and consenting to his death and keeping the garments of those who killed him.

21 Then he said to me, "Go, because I will send you far away to the nations."

22 They were listening to him up to this word, and they lifted up their voices, saying, "Take away such a man from the earth, for it is not fitting for him to live."

23 And as they were crying out and casting off their garments and throwing dust into the air,

24 The captain commanded him to be led into the camp, saying that he should be examined by scourges so that he might recognize for what cause they were crying out against him.

25 As they bound him with straps, Paul said to the centurion who was standing there, "Is it lawful for you to scourge a Roman man who is uncondemned?"

26 When the centurion heard this, he came near and reported to the captain, saying, "See what you are about to do, for this man is a Roman."

27 Then the captain came near and said to him, "Tell me, are you a Roman?" He said, "Yes."

28 The captain answered, "I acquired this commonwealth with a great sum," but Paul said, "But I myself was indeed born a citizen."

29 Immediately therefore those who were about to examine him departed from him, and the captain also was afraid, having recognized that he is a Roman and that he had bound him.

30 On the next day, he willed to know the certainty of what he was accused of by the Jews, loosed him from the bonds, and commanded the high priests and their whole council to come, and bringing Paul down, he stood him among them.


## Chapter 23

1 Gazing at the council, Paul said, "Men, brothers, I have lived my life before God with all good conscience up to this day."

2 Then the high priest Ananias commanded those present to strike his mouth.

3 Then Paul said to him, "God is about to strike you, you whitewashed wall! Do you sit judging me according to the law, yet contrary to law command me to be struck?"

4 But those who were present said, "Are you reviling the high priest of God?"

5 So Paul said, "Brothers, I did not know that he is the high priest, for it is written, 'You will not speak evil of a ruler of your people.'"

6 Knowing then Paul that the one part consists of Sadducees and the other of Pharisees, he cried out in the council, "Men, brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees; for the hope and resurrection of the dead I am being judged."

7 When he had said this, a dissension occurred between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the multitude was split.

8 For the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, and neither an angel nor a Spirit, but the Pharisees confess both.

9 Then a great cry arose, and the scribes of the part of the Pharisees rose up and strove, saying, "We find no evil in this man. But if a spirit or an angel has spoken to him, let us not fight against God."

10 Much dissension having happened, the captain, fearing that Paul would be torn apart by them, commanded the army to go down, snatch him from among them, and lead him into the camp.

11 The following night the Lord stood by him and said, "Be of good cheer, Paul, for as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome."

12 When day came, some of the Jews formed a conspiracy and bound themselves with an oath, saying that they would neither eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.

13 There were more than forty who had formed this conspiracy.

14 They came near to the high priests and the elders and said, "We have bound ourselves with a curse, tasting nothing until we kill Paul."

15 Now therefore you manifest to the captain together with the council, so that tomorrow he may bring him down to you, as you are about to enquire more carefully concerning him; but we, before he draws near, are ready to kill him.

16 When the son of Paul's sister heard of the lying in wait, he came and entered the camp and reported it to Paul.

17 Paul called one of the centurions and said, "Lead this young man away to the captain, for he has something to report to him."

18 Therefore, having received him, he led him to the captain and says, "The prisoner Paul, having called me, asked me to lead this young man to you, as he has something to speak to you."

19 The captain took hold of his hand, withdrew privately, and asked, "What is it that you have to report to me?"

20 He said that the Jews had agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the council tomorrow, as if they were about to inquire more carefully about him.

21 So do not be persuaded by them, for more than forty men among them lie in wait for him, who have bound themselves with an oath neither to eat nor to drink until they kill him; and now they are ready, waiting for the promise from you.

22 So the captain released the young man, commanding him to tell no one that he had manifested these things to me.

23 And having called two of the centurions, he said, "Prepare two hundred soldiers to go as far as Caesarea, and seventy horsemen and two hundred spearmen from the third hour of the night."

24 Present beasts so that, having set Paul on one, they may bring him safely to Felix the governor.

25 having written a letter containing this pattern.

26 Claudius Lysias to the most excellent governor Felix: greetings.

27 This man, having been seized by the Jews and being about to be killed by them, I stood by with the army and delivered him, having learned that he is a Roman.

28 Willing to know the cause for which they were accusing him, he brought him down to their council.

29 whom I found accused about questions of their law, but having no crime laid against him worthy of death or bonds.

30 Having been informed of a plot that was about to happen against the man by the Jews, I sent him to you immediately, commanding the accusers also to say the things against him before you. Farewell.

31 So the soldiers, according to what had been appointed to them, took up Paul and led him by night to Antipatris.

32 On the next day, they allowed the horsemen to go with him and returned to the camp.

33 Those who entered into Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor also presented Paul to him.

34 Then the governor, after reading it and asking what province he was from, and learning that he was from Cilicia,

35 I will hear you, he said, when your accusers also come. And he commanded him to be kept in Herod's praetorium.


## Chapter 24

1 After five days, the high priest Ananias went down with the elders and an orator named Tertullus, who brought before the governor charges against Paul.

2 When he had been called, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, "Since we obtain much peace through you and very worthy deeds take place for this nation through your providence,

3 Always and everywhere we welcome you, most excellent Felix, with all thanksgiving.

4 But so that I may not hinder you further, I encourage you to hear us briefly with your clemency.

5 For we have found this man a pestilence and one who moves dissension among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.

6 who also attempted to profane the temple; and we seized him and wanted to judge him according to our law.

7 But Lysias the captain came upon us and with much violence took him away from our hands.

8 Having commanded the accusers to come before you, you yourself, by examining him, will be able to recognize all these things of which we accuse him.

9 The Jews also agreed, affirming that these things were so.

10 After the governor had beckoned him to speak, Paul answered, "Knowing that you have been a judge over this nation for many years, I defend my case with good cheer."

11 you being able to know that there are not much more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem.

12 and neither in the temple did they find me reasoning with anyone or making a commotion among the crowd, nor in the synagogues, nor throughout the city.

13 Nor can they present anything in the matters of which they now accuse me.

14 I confess this to you, that according to the Way which they call a sect, so I serve the ancestral God, believing all things written in the law and the prophets.

15 having a hope in God, which these men themselves also wait for, that there is about to be a resurrection of the dead, both of the righteous and the unrighteous.

16 In this, I myself exercise to have a blameless conscience toward God and men always.

17 Now after many years I came to do alms for my nation and offerings.

18 In which certain Jews from Asia found me purified in the temple, not with a crowd nor with an uproar.

19 Those who must appear before you and accuse, if they have anything against me.

20 Let these men themselves say what evil doing they found in me while I stood before the council.

21 I am being judged today by you concerning one voice of which I cried out, standing among them, that it is about the resurrection of the dead.

22 When he heard these things, Felix deferred them, knowing more carefully the things concerning the way, saying, "When Lysias the captain comes down, I will enquire about your matter."

23 Having ordered the centurion to keep Paul and to let him have liberty, and to forbid no one of his own people to minister or come near him.

24 After some days, Felix came with Drusilla his wife, who was a Jew, and summoned Paul and heard him concerning faith in Christ.

25 As he reasoned about righteousness and self-control and the judgment about to be, Felix became terrified and answered, "Go for now, but when I have an opportunity, I will summon you."

26 At the same time also hoping that money would be given to him by Paul for him to release him, therefore summoning him more often, he communed with him.

27 When two years had been fulfilled, Felix took Porcius Festus as successor; and wanting to do a favor for the Jews, Felix left Paul bound.


## Chapter 25

1 Festus therefore, having embarked upon the province, after three days went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem.

2 And the high priest and the first of the Jews manifested their case against Paul to him, and they urged him.

3 asking for a favor against him, that he would summon him to Jerusalem, laying an ambush to kill him along the road.

4 So Festus answered that Paul was being kept in Caesarea, and that he himself was about to go out shortly.

5 So those among you who are able, he says, let them go down with me and accuse him if there is anything wrong in this man.

6 After staying among them more than ten days, he went down to Caesarea, and the next day, sitting on the judgment seat, he commanded Paul to be led in.

7 When he had come, the Jews who had gone down from Jerusalem stood around, bringing many and heavy complaints against Paul which they were not able to demonstrate.

8 As he was defending himself, saying, "I have not sinned at all against the law of the Jews or against the temple or against Caesar."

9 But Festus, wanting to do the Jews a favor, answered Paul and said, "Do you want to go up to Jerusalem and be judged there before me about these things?"

10 Paul said, "I am standing before Caesar's judgment seat, where I must be judged. I have done no wrong to the Jews, as you recognize very well."

11 If then I am doing wrong and have done something worthy of death, I do not refuse to die; but if there is nothing to these things of which these men accuse me, no one can hand me over to them; I call upon Caesar.

12 Then Festus, after conversing with the council, answered, "You have appealed to Caesar; to Caesar you shall go."

13 After some days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived at Caesarea to greet Festus.

14 After they had stayed there many days, Festus communicated the case concerning Paul to the king, saying, "There is a man left behind as a prisoner by Felix.

15 When I had arrived in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews presented their case, asking for a punishment against him.

16 To whom I answered that it is not the custom of Romans to forgive any man to destruction before the one being accused might have the accusers face to face and take a place of defense concerning the crime laid against him.

17 When they had come together here, making no delay, on the next day I sat on the judgment seat and commanded the man to be brought.

18 The accusers, standing around him, brought upon [him] no cause of the things which they were thinking.

19 They had some questions against him about their own superstition and about a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul affirmed to be alive.

20 And being at a loss as to the controversy concerning these things, I said whether he would be willing to go to Jerusalem and to be judged there concerning these matters.

21 When Paul had called upon that he be kept for the hearing of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept until I send him to Caesar.

22 Agrippa said to Festus, "I myself also wished to hear the man," but he said, "Tomorrow you will hear him."

23 So on the next day, when Agrippa and Bernice had come with great pomp and had entered the place of hearing together with the captains and prominent men of the city, and when Festus had commanded, Paul was led in.

24 Festus says, "King Agrippa, and all you men who are here present with us, behold this man about whom the whole multitude of the Jews interceded with me, both in Jerusalem and here, crying out that he ought not to live any longer.

25 But when I found that he had done nothing worthy of death, and he himself appealed to Augustus, I decided to send him.

26 It is not certain for me to write anything about him to my lord, so I have brought him before you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that after the examination has taken place, I may have something to write.

27 for it seems unreasonable to me, when sending a prisoner, not also to signify the charges against him.


## Chapter 26

1 Agrippa said to Paul, "You are permitted to speak for yourself." Then Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense.

2 Concerning all the things of which I am accused by the Jews, King Agrippa, I consider myself blessed to be about to defend myself before you today.

3 especially since you are an expert in all the customs and disputes of the Jews; therefore I beg you to hear me patiently.

4 All the Jews know the manner of my life from my youth, which happened from the beginning among my nation in Jerusalem.

5 foreknow me long ago, if they are willing to testify, that according to the most strictest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.

6 Now I stand on trial because of the hope in the promise made by God to our Fathers.

7 To which hope our twelve tribes, serving with earnestness night and day, hope to arrive—concerning which hope I am accused by the Jews, King Agrippa.

8 Why is it judged unbelievable among you that God raises the dead?

9 I indeed thought to myself that I must do many things contrary to the name of Jesus the Nazarene.

10 I also did this in Jerusalem, and I shut up many of the saints in prisons, having received authority from the chief priests, and when they were being killed, I cast my vote against them.

11 And punishing them often in all the synagogues, I compelled them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them even to foreign cities.

12 On which journey, as I was going to Damascus with the authority and commission of the high priests,

13 About midday along the way I saw a king from heaven with a brightness beyond the sun, a light shining around me and those traveling with me.

14 After we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice speaking to me and saying in the Hebrew language, "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the sting."

15 But I said, 'Who are you, Lord?' And he said, 'I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.'

16 But rise and stand on your feet, for this purpose I have appeared to you, to choose you as a servant and witness both of what you have seen and of what I will appear to you.

17 Delivering you from the people and from the nations to whom I now send you.

18 to open their eyes so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the authority of Satan to God, so that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a lot among those who are sanctified by faith in me.

19 Therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision.

20 but first to those in Damascus and Jerusalem, and throughout all the region of Judea, and to the nations, I was reporting that they should repent and turn back to God, practicing deeds worthy of repentance.

21 For these reasons the Jews seized me in the temple and attempted to kill me.

22 Therefore having obtained help from God, I stand to this day testifying to both small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets spoke would happen and what Moses did.

23 Whether the Christ was liable to suffer, whether as the first from the resurrection of the dead He is about to proclaim light to the people and to the nations.

24 And as he was defending himself, Festus said with a loud voice, "You are mad, Paul; your great learning is driving you to madness."

25 But he said, "I am not mad, most excellent Festus, but I speak words of truth and soberness."

26 For the king knows about these things, and I speak boldly to him, because I am not persuaded that any of these things escapes his notice, for this has not been practiced in a corner.

27 King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe.

28 And Agrippa said to Paul, "In a short time you are persuading me to become a Christian."

29 Paul said, "I would pray to God that both in small measure and in great measure, not only you but also all those hearing me today might become such as I am, except for these bonds."

30 When he had said these things, the king rose up, and the governor, and Bernice, and those who were sitting with them.

31 Withdrawing, they spoke to one another, saying, "This man practices nothing worthy of death or bonds."

32 Then Agrippa said to Festus, "This man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar."


## Chapter 27

1 When it was decided that we should sail away to Italy, they delivered both Paul and some other prisoners to a centurion named Julius, of the Augustan cohort.

2 After embarking in a boat of Adramyttium that was about to sail to the places along the coast of Asia, we were led up, Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, being with us.

3 On the next day we were brought down to Sidon; and Julius, having treated Paul courteously, permitted him to go to his friends and obtain care.

4 Having been led up from there, we sailed under Cyprus because the winds were contrary.

5 And sailing over the open sea off Cilicia and Pamphylia, we went down to Myra of Lycia.

6 There the centurion found a boat of Alexandria sailing to Italy, and put us in it.

7 After sufficient days sailing slowly and happening with difficulty off Cnidus, the wind not permitting us, we sailed under Crete along Salmone.

8 And having scarcely passed it, we came to a certain place called Fair Havens, near which was the city of Lasea.

9 After sufficient time had passed and the voyage was already dangerous because the fast had also already passed away, Paul admonished them.

10 Saying to them, "Men, I behold that the voyage is about to be with insult and much loss, not only of the lading and the boat, but also of our souls.

11 But the centurion was being persuaded more by the master and the owner of the ship than by what was said by Paul.

12 Since the harbor was not commodious for wintering in, the majority put forward a plan to set sail from there, if by any means they might be able to arrive at Phoenix, and winter there—a harbor of Crete that looks toward the southwest and northwest.

13 But when a south wind blew softly, supposing they had seized their purpose, they set sail and passed close alongside Crete.

14 But not long after, a tempestuous wind called Euroclydon cast itself against it.

15 When the boat was caught and could not face the wind, we gave way and were carried along.

16 Running under the lee of a small island called Clauda, we were scarcely able to secure the boat.

17 They took up the helps, using them to undergird the boat; and fearing lest they should fall into the quicksands, they let down the vessel and were borne along in this way.

18 We were exceedingly tempest-tossed, so the next day they did a jettison.

19 And on the third day we threw the tackling of the boat overboard with our own hands.

20 And neither sun nor stars appearing for many days, and not a little winter pressing upon us, all remaining hope of our being saved was taken away.

21 Since there was much abstinence from food at that time, Paul stood up in their midst and said, "You men should have hearkened to me and not set sail from Crete, in order to gain this insult and loss."

22 And now I admonish you to be cheerful, for there will be no casting away of life from any of you, except of the boat.

23 For this night there stood by me an angel of the God whose I am, and whom I serve.

24 Saying, "Do not fear, Paul; you must be presented before Caesar, and see, God has granted you all those who sail with you."

25 Therefore be cheerful, men, for I believe God that it will be exactly in the way it was spoken to me.

26 But we must run aground on a certain island.

27 But when the fourteenth night came, as we were being driven back and forth in the Adriatic Sea, about midnight the sailors began to think that some land was being brought near to them.

28 They sounded and found twenty fathoms; then, after departing a little, they sounded again and found fifteen fathoms.

29 Fearing that we might fall away onto rocky places, they threw out four anchors from the stern and prayed for day to happen.

30 When the sailors were seeking to flee from the ship and had let down the boat into the sea under the pretense that they were about to stretch out anchors from the forepart.

31 Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers, "Unless these men remain in the boat, you cannot be saved."

32 Then the soldiers cut off the small cords of the boat and allowed it to fall away.

33 Now while the day was about to begin, Paul encouraged all to partake of food, saying, "Today is the fourteenth day that you have been continuing without eating, having taken nothing yourselves, in expectation."

34 Therefore I encourage you to receive some food, for this is for your salvation; for not a hair from the head of any of you will fall.

35 And having said these things, and having taken bread, he gave thanks to God in the presence of all, and having broken it, he began to eat.

36 All were of good cheer and themselves also received food.

37 And we were in the boat, all the souls, two hundred seventy-six.

38 When they had eaten enough food, they began to lighten the boat by casting out the wheat into the sea.

39 When day came, they did not recognize the land, but they noticed a bay having a shore, onto which they planned, if they were able, to drive the boat.

40 Taking away the anchors, they allowed them into the sea, at the same time releasing the bands of the rudders, and lifting up the mainsail to the blowing wind, they held fast for the shore.

41 Having fallen into a place where two seas meet, they ran the ship aground; and the forepart, sticking fast, remained immovable, but the stern was broken up by the violence of the waves.

42 But the counsel of the soldiers happened, that they should kill the prisoners, lest anyone swim out and escape.

43 But the centurion, willing to save Paul, forbad them of their purpose, and commanded those who were able to swim to cast themselves first into the sea and go out to land.

44 and the rest, some on boards and some on some of the things from the boat, and so it happened that all were saved onto the land.


## Chapter 28

1 And after being saved, they then recognized that the island was called Melita.

2 The barbarians provided us with no ordinary kindness, for they kindled a fire and received all of us because of the rain that was standing by and because of the cold.

3 When Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks and laid them on the fire, a viper came out from the heat and fastened on his hand.

4 Now when the barbarians saw the beast hanging from his hand, they said to one another, "Surely this man is a murderer, whom, though he was saved from the sea, punishment has not allowed to live."

5 So the one who shook off the beast into the fire suffered no ill effect.

6 But they were expecting him to be about to become swollen or suddenly fall down dead. And as they were waiting for a long time and beholding nothing wrong happening to him, they changed their minds and said that he was a god.

7 Now in the areas around that place there were fields belonging to the chief man of the island, named Publius, who received us and courteously entertained us for three days.

8 It happened that the father of Publius was lying sick, constrained by fevers and a bloody flux; Paul went in, prayed, laid his hands on him, and healed him.

9 After this happened, the rest who had weaknesses on the island came near and were healed.

10 who also honored us with many honors, and when we were being taken on board, they put on board the things for the need.

11 After three months we put to sea in a ship that had wintered at the island, an Alexandrian ship with the sign of Castor and Pollux.

12 And having been brought down to Syracuse, we continued there for three days.

13 From there we went around and arrived at Rhegium, and after one day a south wind blew, and on the next day we came to Puteoli.

14 There we found brothers and were encouraged to continue with them for seven days, and so we came to Rome.

15 The brothers there, having heard the news about us, came out to meet us as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns. When Paul saw them, he gave thanks to God and took courage.

16 When we came to Rome, the centurion delivered the prisoners to the captain of the guard, but Paul was permitted to remain by himself with the soldier who kept him.

17 After three days Paul called together the leading men who were among the Jews. When they came together, he said to them, "Men, brothers, I have done nothing contrary to the people or to the ancestral customs. I was delivered as a prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans.

18 They examined me and wanted to release me because no cause of death existed in me.

19 But when the Jews spoke against it, I was compelled to call upon Caesar, not as having anything to accuse my nation of.

20 For this reason therefore I encouraged you to see and to speak with; for because of the hope of Israel I am surrounded with this chain.

21 But they said to him, "We received no letters concerning you from Judea, nor did any of the brothers who have come report or speak anything evil about you."

22 We consider it worthy to hear from you what you think; for indeed, concerning this sect, it is known to us that it is spoken against everywhere.

23 Having appointed a day for him, they came to him at his lodging in even greater numbers, to whom he expounded, testifying about the kingdom of God, and persuading them concerning Jesus from both the Law of Moses and the Prophets, from morning until evening.

24 Some were persuaded by what was said, but others disbelieved.

25 Being in disagreement with one another, they began to depart, after Paul had spoken one word: "The Holy Spirit spoke well through Isaiah the prophet to our fathers."

26 Go to this people and say, "You will keep on hearing but will never understand, and you will keep on looking but will never see."

27 For this people's heart has become dull, and they heard heavily with their ears, and they closed their eyes, lest they see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand with their heart and turn back, and I heal them.

28 Therefore let it be known to you that the salvation of God has been sent to the nations; they will also hear.

29 And while he was saying these things, the Jews departed, having much disputation among themselves.

30 Paul remained two whole years in his own hired house, and welcomed all who entered in to him.

31 preaching the kingdom of God and teaching the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness, no man forbidding him.


# ROMANS

## Chapter 1

1 Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called an apostle, separated for the gospel of God.

2 He promised beforehand through his prophets in the holy Scriptures.

3 concerning His Son, who was born of the seed of David according to the flesh.

4 who was determined to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by the resurrection of the dead, Jesus Christ our Lord.

5 Through whom we received grace and apostleship for the obedience of faith among all the nations, for the sake of his name.

6 among whom you also are called of Jesus Christ.

7 To all who are in Rome, beloved of God, called holy, grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

8 First, I give thanks to my God through Jesus Christ for all of you, because your faith is proclaimed in the whole world.

9 For God is my witness, whom I serve in my spirit in the gospel of his Son, how without ceasing I make remembrance of you.

10 always in my prayers, begging that if by any means I may at last prosper by the will of God to come to you.

11 For I long to see you, so that I may share with you some spiritual gift for the purpose of strengthening you.

12 For this is to be comforted together in you through the faith among one another, both yours and mine.

13 I do not want you to be ignorant, brothers, that I often purposed to come to you, and was forbidden until the present, so that I might have some fruit also among you, just as among the remaining nations.

14 I am a debtor both to Greek and barbarian, both to wise and foolish.

15 Thus, as far as it depends on me, I am ready to preach the gospel to you also who are in Rome.

16 For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.

17 For the righteousness of God is revealed in it from faith to faith, just as it is written: "The righteous one will live by faith."

18 For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men who hold fast the truth in unrighteousness.

19 Because what is known of God is manifest among them, for God revealed it to them.

20 For since the creation of the world his invisible qualities are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, both his eternal power and godhead, so that they are without excuse.

21 For although they knew God, they did not glorify him as God or give thanks, but they became vain in their reasoning, and their senseless heart was darkened.

22 Affirming themselves to be wise, they became foolish.

23 and they changed the glory of the incorruptible God into the likeness of an image of corruptible man and of birds and of fourfooted beasts and of reptiles.

24 Therefore God delivered them over to the desires of their hearts for uncleanness, to dishonor their bodies among themselves.

25 These people changed the truth of God into the lie and worshipped and served the creation rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever. Amen.

26 Therefore God delivered them to dishonorable passions, for even their females changed the natural use into that which is against nature.

27 Likewise also the males left the natural use of the female and burned in their lust toward one another, males with males working shame and receiving in themselves the recompense that was necessary for their error.

28 And just as they did not test to have God in knowledge, God delivered them to a reprobate mind, to do the things that are not convenient.

29 fulfill all unrighteousness, fornication, wickedness, greed, malice, full of envy, murder, strife, deceit, malignity, whisperers

30 backbiters, haters of God, insolent, proud, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents.

31 Senseless, covenant-breakers, without natural affection, implacable, unmerciful.

32 Those who recognize the ordinance of God—that those who practice such things are worthy of death—not only do them but also consent to those who practice them.


## Chapter 2

1 Therefore you are without excuse, O man, every one who judges, for in that which you judge the other, you condemn yourself, for you who judge practice the same things.

2 We know that the judgment of God is according to truth upon those who practice such things.

3 But do you reckon this, O man who judges those who practice such things and does them yourself, that you will escape the judgment of God?

4 Or do you despise the riches of his kindness and forbearance and patience, not knowing that the kindness of God leads you to repentance?

5 But because of your hardness and impenitent heart you are treasuring up for yourself wrath on the day of wrath and revelation of the righteous judgment of God.

6 He will repay each according to his works.

7 To those who seek glory, honor, and incorruption by patience in good work, eternal life.

8 But for those who are of strife and disobey the truth, but are persuaded by unrighteousness, wrath and anger.

9 Tribulation and anguish are upon every soul of man who works the evil, of the Jew first and also of the Greek.

10 But glory, honor, and peace for everyone who works what is good, for the Jew first and also for the Greek.

11 For there is no respect of persons with God.

12 For all who sinned without law will also be destroyed without law, and all who sinned under law will be judged by the law.

13 For the hearers of the law are not righteous before God, but the doers of the law will be justified.

14 For when nations that do not have a law do by nature the things of the law, these, not having a law, are a law to themselves.

15 They show the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience testifying to them, and between one another their thoughts accusing or else defending them.

16 on the day when God judges the secrets of men according to my gospel through Jesus Christ.

17 See, you are called a Jew and rest in the law and boast in God.

18 You know the will and test what differs, being instructed from the law.

19 You persuade yourself to be a guide of the blind, a light for those in darkness.

20 a corrector of the foolish, a teacher of children, having the form of knowledge and of the truth in the law.

21 You then who teach another, do you not teach yourself? You who preach not to steal, do you steal?

22 You who say not to commit adultery, do you commit adultery? You who abhor idols, do you commit sacrilege?

23 You who boast in the law dishonor God through the transgression of the law.

24 For the name of God is blasphemed among the nations because of you, just as it is written.
